name: Professor
summary: A senior academic who teaches advanced students and conducts research—evolving from medieval university masters into the modern tenure-track faculty role.
duties: Lecture, mentor graduate students, publish research, serve on committees, and secure grants.
skills: Subject expertise, pedagogy, writing, peer review, public speaking.
tools: Lecture halls, journals, lab equipment, LMS platforms, citation databases.
regions: Universities worldwide; roots in Bologna, Paris, and Oxford medieval faculties.
educationPath: Doctoral degrees, postdoctoral fellowships, tenure review processes.
modernSectors: Research universities, liberal arts colleges, professional schools, think tanks.

events.label: Research university model
events.description: German research ideals spread professorial expectations beyond teaching alone.

name: Tutor
summary: An educator who instructs individuals or small groups outside mass classrooms—from aristocratic household teachers to modern test-prep coaches.
duties: Customize lessons, assess progress, assign practice, and communicate with parents or sponsors.
skills: Adaptability, patience, subject mastery, scheduling, feedback.
tools: Textbooks, worksheets, video calls, whiteboards, practice exams.
regions: Global; prominent in Britain, East Asia cram schools, and elite households historically.
educationPath: Degrees plus tutoring experience; certification for school-adjacent roles.
modernSectors: Private tutoring agencies, online platforms, homeschooling support, university writing centers.

events.label: Online tutoring
events.description: Video platforms globalize one-to-one instruction beyond local markets.

name: School Principal
summary: The lead administrator of a school who manages staff, safety, budgets, and community relations—descended from headmasters of local grammar schools.
duties: Hire teachers, enforce policy, handle discipline, report to districts, and shape school culture.
skills: Leadership, conflict resolution, budgeting, regulatory compliance, community engagement.
tools: Scheduling systems, safety plans, evaluation rubrics, board meeting agendas.
regions: Public and private school systems worldwide.
educationPath: Teaching experience plus principal licensure and graduate education programs.
modernSectors: K-12 public districts, charter networks, independent schools.

events.label: Federal education expansion
events.description: US Great Society programs increase centralized accountability for principals.

name: Archivist
summary: A specialist who preserves, catalogs, and provides access to historical records—cousin to librarians but focused on unpublished primary sources.
duties: Appraise collections, arrange finding aids, digitize fragile items, and advise researchers.
skills: Cataloging standards, preservation science, reference interviewing, project management.
tools: Acid-free boxes, digitization scanners, DAM systems, climate-controlled stacks.
regions: National archives, universities, corporations, and religious institutions globally.
originLabel: National archives in revolutionary France and successors
educationPath: Archival studies graduate programs, certification through professional societies.
modernSectors: Government archives, university special collections, museum back offices, corporate records.

events.label: Society of American Archivists roots
events.description: Professional standards formalize archivist training in the 20th century.

name: Coach
summary: An instructor who develops athletic skill, fitness, and teamwork—evolving from informal trainers into credentialed school and professional staff.
duties: Plan practices, teach technique, manage game strategy, and safeguard player health protocols.
skills: Motivation, sport-specific tactics, injury awareness, communication with athletes and parents.
tools: Stopwatch, playbook boards, video analysis, training cones, wearable trackers.
regions: School sports worldwide; professional club staff in football, basketball, cricket, and more.
originLabel: Organized school athletics in Britain and US colleges
educationPath: Coaching certifications, CPR and safety training, prior playing experience.
modernSectors: High school athletics, collegiate programs, professional clubs, private academies.

events.label: Modern Olympics revival
events.description: International sport elevates specialized coaching careers.

name: Academic
summary: A scholar employed in higher education or research institutes who advances knowledge through study and publication—broader than professor titles alone.
duties: Conduct studies, write papers, peer-review colleagues, teach when assigned, and attend conferences.
skills: Critical thinking, methodology design, grant writing, collaboration, teaching.
tools: Laboratories, datasets, statistical software, academic networks, preprint servers.
regions: Global university and institute employment since medieval faculties expanded.
educationPath: Advanced degrees, postdoctoral appointments, fellowship competitions.
modernSectors: Universities, national labs, policy institutes, industry R&D liaison roles.

events.label: Journal publishing
events.description: Philosophical Transactions and successors institutionalize scholarly communication.

name: Editor
summary: A publishing professional who shapes manuscripts and news copy for clarity, accuracy, and style—from newspaper desks to book imprints.
duties: Revise drafts, fact-check, manage deadlines, coordinate with writers and designers, and enforce house style.
skills: Language mastery, diplomacy, news judgment, project scheduling, legal sensitivity.
tools: Track changes, style guides, CMS, proof marks, Slack workflows.
regions: Publishing capitals: New York, London, Tokyo, Mumbai.
educationPath: Journalism or English degrees, newsroom apprenticeships, literary editing portfolios.
modernSectors: Newspapers, magazines, book publishers, digital media, technical documentation.

events.label: Penny press editing
events.description: High-volume newsrooms professionalize fast copy editing for mass readers.

name: Publisher
summary: An entrepreneur or executive who finances and distributes books, periodicals, or digital media—combining editorial judgment with business risk.
duties: Acquire titles, set print runs, market releases, manage rights, and oversee editorial staff.
skills: Market sense, negotiation, budgeting, branding, copyright literacy.
tools: Contracts, ISBN systems, marketing analytics, distribution networks, royalty statements.
regions: Global trade publishing; historic centers in Frankfurt, London, and New York.
educationPath: Publishing programs, editorial career progression, entrepreneurship.
modernSectors: Trade books, academic presses, independent imprints, digital-first publishers.

events.label: Copyright expansion
events.description: Statutory copyright frameworks shape publisher business models in Europe and America.

name: Radio Broadcaster
summary: A voice professional who delivered news, drama, and music over AM and FM radio—dominant mass medium before television and streaming.
duties: Read scripts, interview guests, operate boards, time segments to network clocks, and maintain station logs.
skills: Clear diction, improvisation, audio mixing basics, regulatory compliance, audience rapport.
tools: Microphones, mixing consoles, cart machines, later digital playout systems.
regions: US network radio golden age, BBC traditions, local stations worldwide 1920s–2000s.

events.label: Network radio
events.description: NBC and successors nationalize programming and broadcaster careers.

events.label: Television competition
events.description: TV draws advertisers while radio pivots to music formats and local talk.

name: Film Director
summary: A creative lead who interprets scripts and guides actors and crew to produce motion pictures—born with nickelodeons and matured in Hollywood and world cinema.
duties: Block scenes, direct performances, collaborate with cinematographers, supervise edits, and align vision with producers.
skills: Storytelling, leadership, visual composition, budget awareness, negotiation.
tools: Shot lists, monitors, megaphones historically; digital playback and previz today.
regions: Hollywood, Bollywood, Nollywood, European art cinema, East Asian studios.
originLabel: Public cinema screenings after Lumière era
educationPath: Film school, assistant director ladders, independent short film portfolios.
modernSectors: Studios, streaming originals, advertising, music videos, documentary.

events.label: Sound film
events.description: Talkies reshape directing craft and studio employment.

events.label: Streaming platforms
events.description: Netflix-era distribution opens new directing markets beyond theatrical release.

name: Cartoonist
summary: An artist who tells stories or jokes through sequential drawings—flourishing in newspapers, comics pages, and animation studios.
duties: Sketch characters, write gags or plots, meet page deadlines, and adapt styles for print or digital.
skills: Drawing, pacing, satire, consistency, self-editing under deadline.
tools: Ink pens, Bristol board, tablets, Photoshop, animation rigs.
regions: US newspaper strips, Franco-Belgian bande dessinée, Japanese manga industries.
originLabel: Satirical prints and early comic weeklies
educationPath: Art school, portfolio development, syndicate or webcomic audiences.
modernSectors: Newspapers, webcomics, animation studios, graphic novels, editorial cartoons.

events.label: Newspaper comic sections
events.description: Yellow Kid era popularizes daily comic strips as employment for cartoonists.

name: News Anchor
summary: A television presenter who delivers news bulletins to camera—combining journalistic sourcing with performance skills in the broadcast era.
duties: Read teleprompter copy, conduct live interviews, coordinate with producers, and maintain composure during breaking events.
skills: Clear delivery, journalistic ethics, improvisation, appearance management, teamwork in control rooms.
tools: Teleprompter, IFB earpieces, studio cameras, ENPS-style rundown systems.
regions: National networks and local TV markets since mid-20th century.
educationPath: Journalism or communications degrees, reporter-to-anchor career path.
modernSectors: Broadcast TV, cable news, streaming news channels, podcast video simulcasts.

events.label: JFK assassination coverage
events.description: Extended live TV news cements anchor as national authority figure in US media.

name: Winemaker
summary: A vintner who ferments grapes into wine—elevating household craft into estate, cooperative, and industrial winery careers.
duties: Manage harvest, control fermentation, blend batches, barrel-age, and ensure sanitation and labeling compliance.
skills: Enology, tasting, microbiology basics, vineyard coordination, marketing for estates.
tools: Crush pads, fermenters, hydrometers, oak barrels, bottling lines.
regions: Mediterranean antiquity to Bordeaux, Napa, Rioja, and New World regions.
originLabel: Early viticulture in Transcaucasia and Mediterranean
educationPath: Enology degrees, cellar apprenticeships, sommelier-adjacent training.
modernSectors: Estate wineries, cooperatives, bulk wine production, hospitality tie-ins.

events.label: Phylloxera crisis
events.description: Vine pest devastates European vineyards, reshaping rootstock science and labor.

name: Beekeeper
summary: An apiarist who tends honey bee colonies for honey, wax, and pollination services—practiced from ancient Egypt to modern migratory commercial operations.
duties: Inspect hives, manage queens, harvest honey, treat pests, and contract pollination to orchards.
skills: Insect behavior reading, seasonal planning, gentle handling, small business operations.
tools: Smoker, hive tool, protective veil, extractors, migratory truck racks.
regions: Worldwide; commercial pollination hubs in US almond country and Europe.
originLabel: Beekeeping in ancient Egyptian tomb art
educationPath: Apprenticeship, beekeeping associations, agricultural extension courses.
modernSectors: Honey retail, pollination services, wax products, hobbyist markets.

events.label: Colony collapse concern
events.description: Bee die-offs raise scientific and economic attention to commercial beekeeping.

name: Orchardist
summary: A grower who cultivates fruit and nut trees on a commercial scale—specializing beyond general field cropping in perennial horticulture.
duties: Prune trees, manage irrigation, spray for pests responsibly, harvest, and grade produce for market.
skills: Horticulture, equipment operation, seasonal labor coordination, postharvest handling.
tools: Ladders, pruning saws, tractors, sprayers, cold storage rooms.
regions: Apple regions of Washington, citrus belts, olive groves in Mediterranean climates.
educationPath: Horticulture programs, family farm apprenticeship, cooperative extension.
modernSectors: Fresh fruit packing, cider supply, export orchards, agri-tourism.

events.label: Refrigerated rail
events.description: Cold chain shipping expands orchard markets far from growing regions.

name: Dairy Farmer
summary: A livestock producer who raises cattle or other mammals primarily for milk—anchoring butter, cheese, and fluid milk supply chains.
duties: Milk herds, manage breeding, maintain barns, monitor animal health, and meet sanitation regulations.
skills: Animal husbandry, equipment maintenance, record keeping, early-morning discipline.
tools: Milking machines, bulk tanks, hay balers, veterinary supplies.
regions: Wisconsin, Netherlands, New Zealand, Punjab, and pastoral dairy regions globally.
educationPath: Agricultural degrees, cooperative extension, on-farm family training.
modernSectors: Fluid milk, cheese plants, organic dairy, robotic milking operations.

events.label: Centrifugal cream separation
events.description: Industrial dairy technology scales butter and milk processing beyond farm churns.

name: Rancher
summary: A livestock owner who raises cattle or sheep on rangeland—especially associated with American West open-range traditions and modern feedlot finishing.
duties: Rotate pastures, brand or tag stock, arrange sales, manage water rights, and oversee cow-calf operations.
skills: Horsemanship, range ecology, finance, veterinary coordination, drought planning.
tools: Pickup trucks, stock trailers, fencing, branding irons, RFID tags.
regions: US Great Plains, Texas, Argentina pampas, Australian stations.
originLabel: Open-range cattle era in American West
educationPath: Agricultural college, family ranch apprenticeship, grazing management courses.
modernSectors: Beef production, wool, land stewardship contracts, agritourism ranches.

events.label: Barbed wire fencing
events.description: Enclosure ends open-range era and redefines ranch labor and land law.

name: Sharecropper
summary: A tenant farmer who paid rent as a share of the crop rather than cash—widespread in post-emancipation US South and other landlord-tenant regimes.
duties: Plant landlord-assigned crops, maintain fields, deliver agreed share of harvest, and often buy supplies on credit.
skills: Row cropping, debt navigation, endurance, family labor coordination.
tools: Mule plow, hoe, sacks, weigh scales at gin or market.
regions: US South 1870s–1940s peak; parallels in Latin America and Asia.

events.label: Depression and mechanization
events.description: Crop price collapse and tractors displace many marginal tenant farms.

name: Textile Worker
summary: An operator in mills who spun, wove, or finished cloth—central to industrial revolution factory life and still present in export garment hubs.
duties: Tend looms or sewing lines, meet piece rates, maintain quality checks, and follow safety rules in noisy plants.
skills: Machine tending, speed under repetition, basic maintenance, collective organizing historically.
tools: Power looms, sewing machines, cutting tables; historically spinning jenny lines.
regions: Lancashire, Lowell mills, Bangladesh and Vietnam garment zones today.

events.label: Triangle Shirtwaist fire
events.description: Disaster spotlights sweatshop conditions and labor reform in US cities.

name: Factory Worker
summary: A wage laborer in manufacturing plants who operates machinery or assembles products—defining figure of industrial urbanization.
duties: Staff production lines, hit output targets, report defects, and follow safety procedures.
skills: Consistency, teamwork, basic troubleshooting, shift stamina.
tools: Assembly tools, conveyor systems, PPE, MES dashboards in modern plants.
regions: Industrial cities worldwide since 18th-century mills; major Asian manufacturing belts today.
educationPath: On-the-job training, vocational certifications, apprenticeship programs.
modernSectors: Automotive, electronics, food processing, pharmaceuticals, aerospace suppliers.

events.label: Assembly line diffusion
events.description: Ford methods spread segmented tasks and high-volume employment patterns.

name: Steelworker
summary: A metalworker in integrated mills or foundries who produced structural steel—iconic to Pittsburgh, Ruhr, and Tangshan industrial landscapes.
duties: Tend furnaces, cast ingots, roll beams, and work in extreme heat with strict safety protocols.
skills: Heat tolerance, crane signaling, union solidarity traditions, hazard awareness.
tools: Bessemer converters historically; BOF vessels, ladles, rolling mills, hard hats.
regions: US Rust Belt, Germany, China, India steel corridors.

events.label: Basic oxygen steelmaking
events.description: New furnace technology changes mill employment and global competition.

name: Machinist
summary: A precision metalworker who cuts parts on lathes and mills—bridging craft toolmaking and modern CNC manufacturing.
duties: Read blueprints, set up machines, measure tolerances, and produce jigs, shafts, and custom components.
skills: Trigonometry, micrometer reading, CNC programming, problem solving, safety focus.
tools: Lathe, milling machine, calipers, CNC controllers, cutting fluid.
regions: Machine shops in industrial districts worldwide; aerospace and automotive suppliers.
originLabel: Mechanized tool shops in early factories
educationPath: Machinist apprenticeships, trade school, CNC certification courses.
modernSectors: Job shops, defense contractors, medical device manufacturing, prototyping labs.

events.label: CNC adoption
events.description: Computer numeric control shifts machinist work toward programming and setup.

name: Chemical Plant Operator
summary: A technician who runs reactors and distillation units in chemical, petrochemical, and pharmaceutical plants—emerging with industrial chemistry giants.
duties: Monitor control panels, sample batches, adjust flows, log readings, and execute emergency shutdowns.
skills: Process awareness, safety discipline, instrumentation literacy, shift handoffs.
tools: DCS consoles, PPE, gas detectors, sampling ports, lockout-tagout kits.
regions: US Gulf Coast, Rhine chemical corridor, Singapore Jurong, Saudi petrochemical sites.
originLabel: Synthetic dye and acid industrial scale production
educationPath: Process technology associate degrees, operator certification, on-site training.
modernSectors: Petrochemicals, plastics, fertilizers, bulk pharma intermediates.

events.label: Industrial organic chemistry
events.description: BASF-era coal-tar chemistry expands operator employment in large continuous plants.

name: Assembly Worker
summary: A production employee who repetitively joins components on a line—crystallized by automobile and electronics mass manufacturing.
duties: Install parts per sequence, torque fasteners, run functional tests, and escalate defects.
skills: Hand dexterity, pace keeping, quality vigilance, ergonomic awareness.
tools: Power drivers, jigs, conveyor pallets, andon cords, ESD straps for electronics.
regions: Detroit, Toyota-city supply chains, maquiladora zones, Shenzhen electronics corridors.
originLabel: Moving assembly line at Ford Highland Park
educationPath: Employer training, vocational basics, safety orientations.
modernSectors: Automotive final assembly, consumer electronics, appliance plants, aerospace subassembly.

events.label: Ford assembly line
events.description: Highland Park plant divides labor into sequential assembly tasks.
